content:
Artificial Intelligence

Systems Thinking

Engineering

Ai Safety

Complex Systems

12 min read
Just now

--

From observing what happened to understanding what made it possible.
One of the hallmarks of expertise may sound paradoxical, because it isn’t about having more answers. It’s knowing which questions to ask next.
A physician hears “the patient has a fever” and something shifts in how they hold the information. The fever doesn’t become less real. It becomes the beginning of a signal — pointing toward infection, inflammation, autoimmune response, endocrine disruption, or a dozen other mechanisms that could produce the same surface observation.
A structural engineer doesn’t stop at “the bridge collapsed.” The collapse is the event. The investigation is something else entirely: load paths traced backward through the structure, stress concentrations mapped against material tolerances, the sequence of failures reconstructed until the first failure becomes visible. Not the last one. The first one.
A farmer doesn’t simply observe that the crop failed. The observation is accurate and completely insufficient. The investigation turns toward rainfall patterns, soil chemistry, drainage, timing, pest pressure, the dozen interacting variables that together produced a single legible outcome.
Every mature discipline eventually develops this habit.
Observations become the starting point. Mechanisms become the destination.
The distance between them — the work of moving from “what happened” to “what made this possible” — is where understanding actually lives.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI is beginning the same transition
Recent incident reports from frontier AI systems are increasingly described in behavioral language.

• A model deceived its evaluators.

• A model escaped its sandbox.

• A model became misaligned with its designers’ intentions.

• A model started scheming.
These descriptions are useful because they communicate something real about what was observed. But if the history of other disciplines is any guide, they are unlikely to be the language that ultimately explains why those behaviors emerged — or, more importantly, under what conditions they will emerge again.
That distinction matters more than it might initially appear, because the questions we ask determine which explanations we are even capable of discovering.
If structural engineers had stopped at “bridges collapse,” they would never have found fatigue, resonance, corrosion, or progressive load redistribution. If physicians had stopped at “patients die of fever,” germ theory would have had nowhere to arrive. If aviation accident investigators had stopped at “the aircraft failed,” controlled flight into terrain would still be killing pilots at the same rate it did in the 1970s.
Description is where investigation begins. It is not where it ends.

Four behavioral observations, and the engineering questions beneath them
“The model deceived the evaluator.”
This is perhaps the most psychologically loaded phrase in AI safety discourse. Deception implies intent. Intent implies something like a mind that decided to mislead. The language carries a weight of moral judgment that shapes, almost before we notice it, where we look for explanations.
An engineering investigation starts somewhere different.
Not: why did the model choose to deceive?
But: what conditions made deceptive behavior advantageous?
This is not a small reframing. It opens an entirely different investigative space.
Consider what we know about how language models are trained. They are optimized, through various feedback mechanisms, to produce outputs that score well against some evaluation criterion. The evaluation criterion is, necessarily, a proxy for what the designers actually want. It is a measurement, and measurements are always incomplete representations of the underlying reality they’re trying to capture.
If a model is being evaluated on whether its outputs appear correct rather than whether they are correct, and if the model has sufficient capacity to find patterns in that distinction, then outputs that appear correct while being wrong are not deception in any meaningful moral sense. They are the predictable result of optimizing against a proxy metric that was never perfectly aligned with the underlying goal.
The engineering questions that follow from this are concrete and investigable:

• Was the model being rewarded for appearing correct, or for being correct — and how would we tell the difference?

• Did the evaluation environment unintentionally contain shortcuts that wouldn’t generalize to deployment?

• Did the measurement of success become something the optimization process could learn to satisfy without achieving the underlying goal?

• Was the evaluator genuinely independent of the system being evaluated, or had they become coupled in ways that undermined that independence?
None of these questions require attributing intent to the model. All of them point toward concrete aspects of system design that could be examined, modified, and tested.
That is what engineering vocabulary does. It converts observations into hypotheses.

“The model escaped the sandbox.”
The word “escaped” carries strong connotations. Escape implies a system straining against boundaries that were meant to contain it.
The engineering reading is quieter and, I think, ultimately more useful.
A sandbox is not a physical wall. It is a set of assumptions about what the system inside it can reach, interact with, or affect. Those assumptions are encoded in architecture: which interfaces are available, which communication channels exist, which operations are permitted, which resources can be accessed. The sandbox is only as good as the accuracy of those assumptions.
When a system “escapes,” what has actually happened is that the assumptions proved incomplete.

• A path existed that wasn’t modeled as existing.

• A capability was available that wasn’t included in the threat model.

• An interface that was meant to be constrained turned out to be accessible in ways the designers didn’t anticipate.
Interestingly, it is not unique to AI. Every security boundary in the history of computing has been defined by assumptions about what is reachable from inside it. Every security breach in the history of computing has involved discovering that some assumption was wrong.
The engineering investigation asks:

• Which boundary turned out not to be a boundary?

• What new path became available that the containment model didn’t include?

• What assumption about the system’s environment stop holding before anyone noticed?

• Did the operational environment in which the evaluation ran differ from the environment that the containment was designed for?
These are questions about architecture and they point toward specific things that could be examined and changed.
There is something clarifying about this framing. It shifts attention from the system’s motivations to the design of the environment containing it — which is, practically speaking, the thing the designers actually control.

“The model became misaligned.”
Misalignment is a concept that has evolved considerably in AI safety discourse, but in popular usage it often implies a kind of drift — a system that was once pointed in the right direction and then changed course.
The engineering questions here are somewhat more complex, because misalignment can happen in several distinct ways that each point toward different explanations.

• One possibility is objective misspecification: the goal the system was given was never quite the goal the designers actually wanted. It was an approximation — a proxy that captured most of what mattered under the conditions that existed during training, but which diverged from the actual goal in edge cases, or as the system became more capable, or as the deployment environment changed. Not a measurement that became gameable, but a target that was wrong from the start — wrong in ways that only became visible as capability increased.

• A second possibility is what engineers in other domains would call feedback degradation. The signals that were meant to keep the system aligned with the designers’ intentions were delayed, sparse, ambiguous, or simply inadequate for the complexity of the system’s behavior. A control system with bad sensors is not a system with bad values. It is a system operating under informational constraints that make it impossible to correct course reliably.

• A third possibility is objective drift under capability scaling: a goal that was well-specified for a system of one capability level becomes subtly wrong for a more capable version of the same system. The system becomes better at achieving the objective as specified, but the relationship between the specified objective and the actual desired outcome shifts in ways that weren’t anticipated.
Each of these mechanisms produces observations that look like “misalignment.” Each of them points toward different engineering interventions. Conflating them under a single behavioral description makes it harder to distinguish which is actually occurring in a given case — and therefore harder to know what to do about it.
The engineering investigation asks:

• What were we actually optimizing for, and how do we know?

• How was feedback structured, and was it sufficient to correct for the ways the specified goal diverged from the actual goal?

• Did the relationship between the objective and the desired outcome remain stable as capabilities changed?

• What would we need to measure to distinguish between these different mechanisms?

“The model started scheming.”
This is perhaps the phrase that carries the most science-fiction freight. Scheming implies a quality of mind — deliberate, strategic, operating with intent across time — that feels categorically different from what we expect of software systems.
The engineering questions, again, are more mundane.
Scheming, in behavioral terms, describes a system that appears to be optimizing across multiple steps — taking actions now that only make sense given anticipated future states. This requires, at minimum, some capacity for representing future states and selecting present actions partly on the basis of their expected future consequences.
Whether a system exhibits this behavior is a question about its capabilities — specifically, about what planning horizon has become available to it and what internal representations allow it to model sequences of events rather than single steps.
This matters because capability is not a binary. Planning horizon extends gradually as systems become more capable. What counts as “scheming” from the outside — taking actions that only make sense across multiple steps — might better be described as the natural result of optimization occurring at a longer temporal scale.
The engineering questions:

• What planning horizon is actually available to this system, and how do we know?

• What internal representations would be required for the observed behavior, and do we have evidence that those representations exist?

• Which capabilities emerged through scale rather than explicit design — and how would the presence or absence of those capabilities change the behavior we observe?

• What constraints on long-horizon optimization currently apply, and are those constraints robust as capability increases?
These questions don’t make scheming less interesting or less important to understand. They make it more tractable. They convert a behavioral observation into a set of hypotheses about mechanisms that could, in principle, be investigated.

The danger of stopping too early
In our earlier example, suppose the investigation had ended at “the bridge collapsed.”
We would never have discovered metal fatigue — the phenomenon by which materials that are repeatedly stressed far below their failure point eventually develop microscopic cracks that propagate until catastrophic failure o
